claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin. To determine your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your child or children.

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because eligibility depends on age:
- Children **under 16** generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** don't qualify for Child Benefit

Please tell me your child's age, and if you have multiple children you'd like to check eligibility for, let me know about all of them.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'm interested in finding out if I can claim Child Benefit. I have one child - my son Alex, he's 17 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So you have one child - Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is 17 (which falls in the 16-19 age range), his eligibility will depend on whether he's in approved education or training. Let me navigate to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to know about Alex's current situation regarding education or training.

**Is Alex currently:**

1. **In approved education or training** (currently enrolled and attending), OR
2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last 20 weeks), OR  
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave

Which situation applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is currently in education - he's studying for his A-levels at sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since Alex is currently in education studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, let me check the next requirement about the level and type of education.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are **approved (non-advanced) education** for Child Benefit purposes. Now I need to check a few more details about Alex's education:

**Questions about Alex's A-level studies:**

1. **Is his course full-time?** (At least 12 hours per week of supervised study - this typically includes classroom time, but not homework or private study)

2. **Is this education funded by an employer or linked to a future job agreement with an employer?** (Standard sixth form A-levels usually aren't, but I need to confirm)

3. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?** (This only matters if he is, as married 16-19 year olds must be financially dependent on you to qualify)

Please let me know about these points.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it's a full-time course at sixth form - he's there during the week like any normal school schedule. No, it's not funded by an employer or anything like that, it's just regular sixth form. And no, he's not married.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Alex's situation meets all the requirements so far:
- ✓ He's studying A-levels (approved non-advanced education)
- ✓ Full-time course (12+ hours per week)
- ✓ Not employer-funded
- ✓ Not married

Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about Alex's work situation.

**Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week?** (Or is he not in paid work at all?)

This is important because children aged 16+ who work 24 hours or more per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they're in education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work at all - he's just focused on his studies at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex isn't working, so that requirement is met. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about benefits.

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?** Specifically, is he receiving:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA), or
- Universal Credit

in his own name?

(If he's not receiving any of these benefits, the answer is "no, he's not receiving these benefits")

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not receiving any benefits in his own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about apprenticeships.

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

If he is, I need to know which type and where (England, Scotland, or Wales), as the rules vary by region:
- English apprenticeships generally disqualify from Child Benefit
- Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify
- Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are approved, but other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify

From what you've told me, it sounds like he's just doing regular A-levels at sixth form, not an apprenticeship - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - he's just doing regular A-levels at sixth form, not an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is not in an apprenticeship, so that's fine. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check your (the claimant's) residency status, as you need to meet UK residency requirements to claim Child Benefit.

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If yes, that's the simplest path. If not, there are other ways you might qualify (such as having settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, being a Crown servant abroad, etc.), but let's start with the basic question:

Do you normally live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the residency requirement by normally living in the UK. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check if you're responsible for Alex. You can be considered responsible in any of these ways:

1. **You live with Alex**, OR
2. **You contribute to his upkeep** (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit would provide - through food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.), OR
3. **You pay maintenance** under a court order or agreement

**Do any of these apply to you?** 

The most common situation is that you live with your child - is that the case with Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You're responsible for Alex since you live together. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good! Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for a child at a time. If someone else is claiming, they would need to stop their claim before you could claim.

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! No one else is claiming for Alex. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about Alex's living situation. 

**Is Alex currently:**

1. **In hospital** (long-term stay), OR
2. **In local authority care**, OR
3. **In foster care**, OR
4. **Living normally** (at home with you, which is what it sounds like)

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living at home with you normally - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct - he's living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is living at home with you normally. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Just one final check:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

This would be things like the council paying for his living costs or accommodation. In most normal situations where a child lives at home with their parent, the answer would be no.

Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me proceed to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News! You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

**Result: ELIGIBLE**

You meet all the Child Benefit eligibility requirements for your son Alex.

### Summary for Alex:
✓ **Age**: 17 years old (16-19 age group)  
✓ **Education**: Full-time A-levels at sixth form college (approved non-advanced education)  
✓ **Work status**: Not working (under 24 hours per week)  
✓ **Benefits**: Not receiving ESA or Universal Credit in his own right  
✓ **Apprenticeship**: Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship  
✓ **Your residency**: You live in the UK  
✓ **Responsibility**: You live with Alex  
✓ **Other claimants**: No one else is claiming for Alex  
✓ **Living situation**: Living at home normally  
✓ **Council support**: Council not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (though you can still claim)
5. **Payments**: You'll receive payments every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**How to claim**: Visit [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about claiming Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and studying full-time A-levels at sixth form college, which is approved non-advanced education. He is not working (less than 24 hours per week), not receiving benefits in his own right (ESA or Universal Credit), and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (meeting residency requirements), is responsible for Alex (they live together), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living at home normally, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 17-year-old son Alex who is in full-time approved education (A-levels) at sixth form college.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 17 years old and studying full-time A-levels at sixth form college, which is approved non-advanced education. He is not working (less than 24 hours per week), not receiving benefits in his own right (ESA or Universal Credit), and not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant lives in the UK (meeting residency requirements), is responsible for Alex (they live together), no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, Alex is living at home normally, and the local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance. All eligibility criteria are met.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements for their 17-year-old son Alex who is in full-time approved education (A-levels) at sixth form college.
